did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), specifically a proposed Learn Resized Recurrent Residual Convolutional Neural Network (LR3U-Net) based on R2U-Net with a Learnable Image Resizer, to perform skin cancer segmentation tasks on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It also uses DCNNs (ResNet50) for Diabetic Retinopathy classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, proposing two new Deep Convolutional Neural Network models (LR3U-Net and a classification model with Learnable Image Resizer) for medical image segmentation and classification. It details specific methodologies, datasets (ISIC 2018, APTOS 2019, IDRiD), implementation details, and reports quantitative results (accuracy, IoU) comparing the proposed models against state-of-the-art baselines. The title 'Empirical Analysis' and the structure (Abstract, Introduction, Related Works, Methodology, Results, Conclusion) confirm it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Empirical Analysis of Learnable Image Resizer For Large-Scale Medical Image Classification And Segmentation
journal: IEEE National Aerospace and Electronics Conference
brief_summary: This study proposes two Deep Convolutional Neural Network models incorporating a Learnable Image Resizer to address challenges in processing large-scale medical images for classification and segmentation tasks. The classification model targets Diabetic Retinopathy grading using the APTOS 2019 and IDRiD datasets, while the segmentation model, LR3U-Net, targets skin cancer segmentation using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The proposed classification architecture achieved accuracy improvements of 5.34% and 7.39% over the base ResNet50 model at different input resolutions. For skin cancer segmentation, the LR3U-Net model demonstrated superior performance, yielding approximately 0.62% higher accuracy and 0.28% higher Intersection-over-Union compared to state-of-the-art methods. The experimental results indicate that the proposed models enable better learning with fewer training examples and handle high-resolution inputs more effectively than existing approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper performs classification of Diabetic Retinopathy using the APTOS 2019 and IDRiD datasets. The specific classes/stages studied are No DR, Mild DR, Moderate DR, Severe DR, and Proliferative DR, graded according to the International Clinical DR Disease Severity Scale (ICDRSS).

The paper performs segmentation of skin cancer lesions using the ISIC 2018 dataset. While the task is broadly 'skin cancer segmentation', the introduction specifically mentions Melanoma as the deadliest form of skin cancer. The ISIC 2018 dataset typically contains multiple lesion types (e.g., melanoma, nevus, BCC, etc.), but the paper treats the segmentation task as a binary or multi-class segmentation of 'skin cancer' lesions without explicitly listing the specific histopathological subtypes used in the ISIC 2018 ground truth masks in the provided text.

all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of Diabetic Retinopathy into five discrete categories: No DR, Mild DR, Moderate DR, Severe DR, and Proliferative DR, based on the International Clinical DR Disease Severity Scale (ICDRSS). The model uses Sparse Categorical Cross entropy as the loss function and achieves accuracy improvements over baseline ResNet50.

Binary segmentation of skin cancer lesions from dermoscopic images using the LR3U-Net model on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The task involves predicting pixel-wise masks for the affected area, evaluated using Intersection-over-Union (IoU), accuracy, precision, and recall, with binary cross-entropy as the loss function.

datasets_used.name: APTOS 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used for Diabetic Retinopathy classification, consisting of high-resolution retinal fundus images.

use.training: Used for training the Diabetic Retinopathy classification model (ResNet50 with Learnable Image Resizer). Data augmentation (flips, rotations) was applied to address class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing the Diabetic Retinopathy classification model.

datasets_used.name: IDRiD
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used for Diabetic Retinopathy classification, consisting of high-resolution retinal fundus images graded according to the International Clinical DR Disease Severity Scale (ICDRSS).

use.training: Used for training the Diabetic Retinopathy classification model. Data augmentation was applied.
use.testing: Used for testing the Diabetic Retinopathy classification model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used for skin cancer segmentation, consisting of dermoscopic images with corresponding ground truth masks.

use.training: Used for training the LR3U-Net segmentation model. A subset of 1000 images was randomly selected for fair comparison at 512x512 resolution due to memory limitations.
use.validation: Used for validation to select the best model during training.
use.testing: Used for testing the segmentation model performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, IoU, precision, sensitivity) without statistical significance analysis.

APTOS 2019.notes: The proposed model improved accuracy by 5.34% over the baseline at 512x512 resolution and by 7.39% over other state-of-the-art methods at 1024x1024 resolution. Training time for baseline was 53 seconds/epoch, while the proposed model took 39 seconds/epoch at 1024x1024 input size (97 seconds/epoch with increment of input size mentioned in text, but 39s is explicitly stated for learn resizer vs 53s baseline). Note: Text states 'training time was 39 seconds' for learn resizer vs '53 seconds' for baseline, and '97 seconds' for 1024x1024 input training time. The 87.68% accuracy is the best achieved.

IDRiD.notes: The paper states that APTOS 2019 and IDRiD datasets were considered for the DR experiment. However, specific quantitative accuracy results for IDRiD are not explicitly listed in the provided tables or text; the reported accuracy improvements (5.34% and 7.39%) and specific accuracy values (83.71%, 87.68%) are associated with the APTOS 2019 dataset results in Table III.

ISIC 2018.notes: Results are presented for two input resolutions (256x256 and 512x512). The proposed LR3U-Net outperformed the baseline R2U-Net at both resolutions. In comparison to other state-of-the-art architectures (Table IV), LR3U-Net achieved an IoU of 0.8228, which is higher than R2U-Net (0.8204), FAT-Net (0.820), and others listed. The paper notes that the proposed model yielded around 0.62% higher accuracy and 0.28% higher IoU compared to state-of-the-art performance in the abstract, likely referring to the specific comparison against R2U-Net or the general SOTA group.

preprocessing_techniques: Learnable Image Resizer (LIR) using a shallow CNN network with a ResNet block and bilinear filters to resize high-resolution images while preserving trainable features.
Data augmentation techniques including left-to-right flip, upside-down flip, and ninety-degree rotation to address inter-class imbalance.
Resizing images to fixed resolutions (1024x1024, 512x512, 256x256, 224x224) to reduce computation complexity and fit hardware constraints.

Patch extraction/patching approaches mentioned as a common baseline method for embedding large-scale images into lower-dimensional spaces (though the proposed method avoids this for global context preservation).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Left-to-right flip, upside-down flip, and ninety-degree rotation were applied to address inter-class imbalance in the APTOS 2019 and IDRiD datasets. Specific augmentation counts per class are reported in Table II (e.g., for APTOS 2019 Class 1: 1080 augmented images; Class 3: 747 augmented images; Class 4: 652 augmented images).

conclusions: The proposed classification model, combining a Learnable Image Resizer with ResNet50, achieved a 7.39% improvement in accuracy over state-of-the-art methods when processing 1024x1024 input images for Diabetic Retinopathy classification.
The proposed classification model achieved a 5.34% improvement in accuracy over the baseline ResNet50 model at 512x512 resolution.

The proposed LR3U-Net segmentation model for skin cancer achieved approximately 0.62% higher accuracy and 0.28% higher Intersection-over-Union (IoU) compared to state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

The proposed models enable better learning with a comparatively lower number of training examples, handling high-resolution inputs more effectively than existing approaches that rely on patching or fixed low-resolution resizing.
The LR3U-Net architecture can handle higher-resolution input images and perform very close to existing methods even when the training data is less than half the size typically used.
The proposed classification model was effectively trained with 1024x1024 dimensional inputs, which was not feasible without the proposed resizer network due to computational memory limitations.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on public datasets (APTOS 2019, IDRiD, ISIC 2018) without testing on independent, multi-center clinical data.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons are based on absolute metric values only.

The skin cancer segmentation experiment on ISIC 2018 used a randomly selected subset of 1000 images for testing and a subset of 2100 for training due to memory limitations, which may not represent the full dataset distribution.
The Diabetic Retinopathy classification results for the IDRiD dataset are not explicitly reported in the provided text; only APTOS 2019 results are detailed.
The paper does not report standard deviation or variance for the reported accuracy and IoU metrics, limiting the assessment of result stability.
The proposed models were evaluated on a specific hardware configuration (Intel i9-12900K CPU, NVIDIA RTX3080 GPU), and performance gains in training time may not generalize to other hardware setups.
Data augmentation was applied to address class imbalance in the APTOS 2019 and IDRiD datasets, but the specific impact of this augmentation on model generalizability is not analyzed.

The study claims the proposed method handles high-resolution inputs better, but the comparison with state-of-the-art methods for segmentation is limited to specific resolutions (256x256 and 512x512) and does not test the full range of original image resolutions.
